Res. 14, under a closed rule. The joint resolutions are debated for one hour each and the concurrent resolution is debated for 3 hours.

          },
          "proposedDate": "2025-04-04T04:00:00Z",
          "purpose": "To establish a deficit-neutral reserve fund relating to reversing cuts to the Social Security Administration, which may include cuts ordered by the Department of Government Efficiency or any other cuts to seniors' services.",

            },
            "text": "Amendment SA 2177 not agreed to in Senate by Yea-Nay Vote. 48 - 51. Record Vote Number: 188."
          },
          "number": "2177",
          "onBehalfOfSponsor": {
            "item": {
              "bioguideId": "W000790",
              "firstName": "Raphael",
              "fullName": "Sen. Warnock, Raphael G. [D-GA]",
              "lastName": "Warnock",
              "party": "D",
              "state": "GA",
              "type": "Proposed on behalf of"
            }
          },
          "proposedDate": "2025-04-05T04:00:00Z",
          "purpose": "To establish a deficit-neutral reserve fund relating to access to health care, which may include legislation preventing reductions in funding for Medicaid that could lead to benefit cuts, coverage loss, or slashed provider payments.",
          "sponsors": {
            "item": {
              "bioguideId": "W000790",

              "fullName": "Sen. Warnock, Raphael G. [D-GA]",

              "party": "D",
              "state": "GA"
            }
          },

          "proposedDate": "2025-04-04T04:00:00Z",
          "purpose": "To establish a deficit-neutral reserve fund relating to preventing the use of proceeds from public land sales to reduce the Federal deficit.",

          },
          "proposedDate": "2025-04-04T04:00:00Z",
          "purpose": "To create a point of order against legislation that would create more debt over a 30-year period than has accumulated over the past 249 years.",

          },
          "number": "1693",
          "onBehalfOfSponsor": {
            "item": {
              "bioguideId": "B001230",

          },
          "proposedDate": "2025-04-05T04:00:00Z",
          "purpose": "To establish a deficit-neutral reserve fund relating to preventing a reduction in Medicaid funding that could lead to rural hospital closures, cost increases for individuals with other kinds of insurance, or higher rates of uncompensated care.",
          "sponsors": {

              "bioguideId": "B001230",

        {

              "item": [
                {
                  "actionDate": "2025-04-05",
                  "sourceSystem": {
                    "code": "0",
                    "name": "Senate"
                  },
                  "text": "Amendment SA 1690 ruled out of order by the chair.",
                  "type": "Floor"
                },
                {
                  "actionDate": "2025-04-05",
                  "recordedVotes": {
                    "recordedVote": {
                      "chamber": "Senate",
                      "congress": "119",
                      "date": "2025-04-05T05:16:21Z",
                      "rollNumber": "185",
                      "sessionNumber": "1",
                      "url": "https://www.senate.gov/legislative/LIS/roll_call_votes/vote1191/vote_119_1_00185.xml"
                    }
                  },
                  "sourceSystem": {
                    "code": "0",
                    "name": "Senate"
                  },
                  "text": "Motion to waive all applicable budgetary discipline with respect to amendment SA 1690 rejected in Senate by Yea-Nay Vote. 49 - 50. Record Vote Number: 185.",
                  "type": "Floor"
                },
                {
                  "actionDate": "2025-04-05",
                  "sourceSystem": {
                    "code": "0",
                    "name": "Senate"
                  },
                  "text": "Point of order that the amendment violates section 305(b)(2) of the CBA raised in Senate with respect to amendment SA 1690.",
                  "type": "Floor"
                },
                {
                  "actionDate": "2025-04-05",
                  "sourceSystem": {
                    "code": "0",
                    "name": "Senate"
                  },
                  "text": "Amendment SA 1690 proposed by Senator Cortez Masto to Amendment SA 1717. (consideration: CR S2355-2356)",
                  "type": "Floor"
                },
                {

          "latestAction": {
            "actionDate": "2025-04-05",
            "links": {
              "link": {
                "name": "SA 1661",
                "url": "https://www.congress.gov/amendment/119th-congress/senate-amendment/1661"
              }
            },
            "text": "Amendment SA 1661 not agreed to in Senate by Voice Vote."
          },
          "number": "1661",
          "onBehalfOfSponsor": {
            "item": {
              "bioguideId": "W000800",
              "firstName": "Peter",
              "fullName": "Rep. Welch, Peter [D-VT-At Large]",
              "lastName": "Welch",
              "party": "D",
              "state": "VT",
              "type": "Proposed on behalf of"
            }
          },
          "proposedDate": "2025-04-05T04:00:00Z",
          "purpose": "To create a point of order against legislation that defunds essential services for children, families, and seniors, including programs that feed hungry seniors like Meals on Wheels, Head Start and other child care assistance that allows parents to work and pay their bills, and programs that keep children safe from abuse and neglect, to give massive tax cuts to billionaires.",
          "sponsors": {
            "item": {
              "bioguideId": "W000800",
              "firstName": "Peter",
              "fullName": "Sen. Welch, Peter [D-VT]",
              "lastName": "Welch",
              "party": "D",
              "state": "VT"
            }
          },
          "submittedDate": "2025-04-03T04:00:00Z",
          "textVersions": {
            "count": "1"
          },
          "type": "SAMDT",
          "updateDate": "2025-04-08T11:08:20Z"
        },
        {
          "actions": {

              "item": [
                {
                  "actionCode": "Intro-S",
                  "actionDate": "2025-04-03",

          "proposedDate": "2025-04-04T04:00:00Z",
          "purpose": "To establish a deficit-neutral reserve fund relating to prohibiting attacks on Federal employees by protecting legally binding collective bargaining agreements and the right to organize.",

            "text": "Amendment SA 1310 not agreed to in Senate by Yea-Nay Vote. 46 - 53. Record Vote Number: 174."
          },
          "number": "1310",
          "onBehalfOfSponsor": {
            "item": {
              "bioguideId": "W000805",
              "firstName": "Mark",
              "fullName": "Sen. Warner, Mark R. [D-VA]",
              "lastName": "Warner",
              "party": "D",
              "state": "VA",
              "type": "Proposed on behalf of"
            }
          },
          "proposedDate": "2025-04-04T04:00:00Z",
          "purpose": "To establish a deficit-neutral reserve fund relating to strengthening protections for members of the Armed Forces by prohibiting the use of any commercial messaging application to transmit information revealing the timing, sequencing, or weapons to be used during impending United States military operations in foreign countries that may endanger the lives of members of the Armed Forces.",
          "sponsors": {
            "item": {
              "bioguideId": "W000805",
              "firstName": "Mark",
              "fullName": "Sen. Warner, Mark R. [D-VA]",
              "lastName": "Warner",
              "middleName": "R.",
              "party": "D",
              "state": "VA"
            }
          },
          "submittedDate": "2025-04-03T04:00:00Z",
          "textVersions": {
            "count": "1"
          },
          "type": "SAMDT",
          "updateDate": "2025-04-08T11:08:20Z"
        },
        {
          "actions": {

{

      "text": "IV\nUnion Calendar No. 1\n119th CONGRESS\n1st Session\nH. CON. RES. 14\n[Report No. 119\u20134]\nIN THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES\nFebruary 18, 2025 Mr. Arrington , from the Committee on the Budget , reported the following concurrent resolution; which was committed to the Committee of the Whole House on the State of the Union and ordered to be printed\nCONCURRENT RESOLUTION\nEstablishing the congressional budget for the United States Government for fiscal year 2025 and setting forth the appropriate budgetary levels for fiscal years 2026 through 2034.\n#### 1. Concurrent resolution on the budget for fiscal year 2025\n##### (a) Declaration\nThe Congress determines and declares that prior concurrent resolutions on the budget are replaced as of fiscal year 2025 and that this concurrent resolution establishes the budget for fiscal year 2025 and sets forth the appropriate budgetary levels for fiscal years 2026 through 2034.\n##### (b) Table of contents\nThe table of contents for this concurrent resolution is as follows:\nSec. 1. Concurrent resolution on the budget for fiscal year 2025.\nTitle I\u2014Recommended Levels and Amounts\nSec. 1001. Recommended levels and amounts.\nSec. 1002. Major functional categories.\nTitle II\u2014Reconciliation and Related Matters\nSec. 2001. Reconciliation in the House of Representatives.\nTitle III\u2014Reserve Fund\nSec. 3001. Reserve fund for reconciliation legislation in the House of Representatives.\nSec. 3002. Adjustment for spending cuts of at least $2 trillion.\nTitle IV\u2014Policy Statements\nSec. 4001. Policy statement on economic growth.\nSec. 4002. Policy statement on mandatory spending reduction.\nSec. 4003. Policy statement on Government deregulation.\nTitle V\u2014Other matters\nSec. 5001. Enforcement filing in the House of Representatives.\nSec. 5002. Budgetary treatment of administrative expenses in the House of Representatives.\nSec. 5003. Application and effect of changes in allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels.\nSec. 5004. Adjustments to reflect changes in concepts and definitions in the House of Representatives.\nSec. 5005. Adjustment for changes in the baseline.\nSec. 5006. Exercise of rulemaking powers.\nI\nRecommended Levels and Amounts\n#### 1001. Recommended levels and amounts\nThe following budgetary levels are appropriate for each of fiscal years 2025 through 2034:\n**(1) Federal revenues**\nFor purposes of the enforcement of this concurrent resolution:\n**(A)**\nThe recommended levels of Federal revenues are as follows:\nFiscal year 2025: $3,408,969,000,000.\nFiscal year 2026: $3,766,668,000,000.\nFiscal year 2027: $4,066,393,000,000.\nFiscal year 2028: $4,186,847,000,000.\nFiscal year 2029: $4,309,831,000,000.\nFiscal year 2030: $4,508,641,000,000.\nFiscal year 2031: $4,730,270,000,000.\nFiscal year 2032: $4,938,712,000,000.\nFiscal year 2033: $5,172,643,000,000.\nFiscal year 2034: $5,410,030,000,000.\n**(B)**\nThe amounts by which the aggregate levels of Federal revenues should be changed are as follows:\nFiscal year 2025: -$450,000,000,000.\nFiscal year 2026: -$450,000,000,000.\nFiscal year 2027: -$450,000,000,000.\nFiscal year 2028: -$450,000,000,000.\nFiscal year 2029: -$450,000,000,000.\nFiscal year 2030: -$450,000,000,000.\nFiscal year 2031: -$450,000,000,000.\nFiscal year 2032: -$450,000,000,000.\nFiscal year 2033: -$450,000,000,000.\nFiscal year 2034: -$450,000,000,000.\n**(2) New budget authority**\nFor purposes of the enforcement of this concurrent resolution, the appropriate levels of total new budget authority are as follows:\nFiscal year 2025: $5,515,610,000,000.\nFiscal year 2026: $5,605,352,000,000.\nFiscal year 2027: $5,744,975,000,000.\nFiscal year 2028: $5,999,399,000,000.\nFiscal year 2029: $6,173,475,000,000.\nFiscal year 2030: $6,494,898,000,000.\nFiscal year 2031: $6,748,868,000,000.\nFiscal year 2032: $7,048,096,000,000.\nFiscal year 2033: $7,438,116,000,000 .\nFiscal year 2034: $7,610,582,000,000.\n**(3) Budget outlays**\nFor purposes of the enforcement of this concurrent resolution, the appropriate levels of total budget outlays are as follows:\nFiscal year 2025: $5,490,790,000,000.\nFiscal year 2026: $5,623,085,000,000.\nFiscal year 2027: $5,821,621,000,000.\nFiscal year 2028: $6,088,332,000,000.\nFiscal year 2029: $6,164,293,000,000.\nFiscal year 2030: $6,484,545,000,000.\nFiscal year 2031: $6,720,491,000,000.\nFiscal year 2032: $6,983,637,000,000.\nFiscal year 2033: $7,401,699,000,000.\nFiscal year 2034: $7,529,256,000,000.\n**(4) Deficits**\nFor purposes of the enforcement of this concurrent resolution, the amounts of the deficits are as follows:\nFiscal year 2025: $2,081,821,000,000.\nFiscal year 2026: $1,856,417,000,000.\nFiscal year 2027: $1,755,228,000,000.\nFiscal year 2028: $1,901,485,000,000.\nFiscal year 2029: $1,854,462,000,000.\nFiscal year 2030: $1,975,904,000,000.\nFiscal year 2031: $1,990,221,000,000.\nFiscal year 2032: $2,044,925,000,000.\nFiscal year 2033: $2,229,056,000,000.\nFiscal year 2034: $2,119,226,000,000.\n**(5) Debt subject to limit**\nThe appropriate levels of debt subject to limit are as follows:\nFiscal year 2025: $37,660,656,000,000.\nFiscal year 2026: $39,839,449,000,000.\nFiscal year 2027: $41,752,932,000,000.\nFiscal year 2028: $43,721,320,000,000.\nFiscal year 2029: $45,725,094,000,000.\nFiscal year 2030: the public**\nThe appropriate levels of debt held by the public are as follows:\nFiscal year 2025: $30,430,405,000,000.\nFiscal year 2026: $32,469,082,000,000.\nFiscal year 2027: -$145,400,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, -$145,407,000,000.\nFiscal year 2030:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$149,582,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, -$149,581,000,000.\nFiscal year 2031:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$154,014,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, -$154,013,000,000.\nFiscal year 2032:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$160,114,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, -$160,113,000,000.\nFiscal year 2033:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$166,102,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, -$166,101,000,000.\nFiscal year 2034:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$171,015,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays,-$171,014,000,000.\n**(22)**\nAcross-the-Board Adjustment (990):\nFiscal year 2025:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$4,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nFiscal year 2026:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$4,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nFiscal year 2027:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$4,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nFiscal year 2028:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$4,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nFiscal year 2029:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$4,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nFiscal year 2030:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$4,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nFiscal year 2031:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$4,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nFiscal year 2032:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$4,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nFiscal year 2033:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$5,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nFiscal year 2034:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$5,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nII\nReconciliation and Related Matters\n#### 2001. Reconciliation in the House of Representatives\n##### (a) Submissions\nNot later than March 27, 2025, the committees named in subsection (b) and subsection (c) shall submit their recommendations on changes in laws within their jurisdictions to the Committee on the Budget of the House of Representatives to carry out this section.\n##### (b) Instructions\n**(1) Committee on Agriculture**\nThe Committee on Agriculture shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $230,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(2) Committee on Armed Services**\nThe Committee on Armed Services shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the deficit by not more than $100,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(3) Committee on Education and Workforce**\nThe Committee on Education and Workforce shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $330,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(4) Committee on Energy and Commerce**\nThe Committee on Energy and Commerce shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $880,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(5) Committee on Financial Services**\nThe Committee on Financial Services shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $1,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(6) Committee on Homeland Security**\nThe Committee on Homeland Security shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the deficit by not more than $90,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(7) Committee on the Judiciary**\nThe Committee on the Judiciary shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the deficit by not more than $110,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(8) Committee on Natural Resources**\nThe Committee on Natural Resources shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $1,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(9) Committee on Oversight and Government Reform**\nThe Committee on Oversight and Government Reform shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $50,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(10) Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure**\nThe Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $10,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(11) Committee on Ways and Means**\nThe Committee on Ways and Means shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the deficit by not more than $4,500,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n##### (c) Increase in statutory debt limit\nThe Committee on Ways and Means shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the statutory debt limit by $4,000,000,000,000.\nIII\nReserve Fund\n#### 3001. Reserve fund for reconciliation legislation in the House of Representatives\n##### (a) In general\nIn the House of Representatives, the chair of the Committee on the Budget may revise the allocations of a committee or committees, aggregates, and other appropriate levels in this resolution for any bill or joint resolution considered pursuant to section 2001 containing the recommendations of one or more committees, or for one or more amendments to, a conference report on, or an amendment between the Houses in relation to such a bill or joint resolution, by the amounts necessary to accommodate the budgetary effects of the legislation, if the budgetary effects of the legislation comply with the reconciliation instructions under this concurrent resolution.\n##### (b) Determination of compliance\nFor purposes of this section, compliance with the reconciliation instructions under this concurrent resolution shall be determined by the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House of Representatives.\n#### 3002. Adjustment for spending cuts of at least $2 trillion\n##### (a) Adjustment if deficit reduction target not achieved\nIf one or more committees of the House of Representatives submit reconciliation recommendations pursuant to paragraphs (1), (3), (4), (5), (8), (9), or (10) of section 2001(b) and such recommendations do not, in total, achieve at least $2,000,000,000,000 in net deficit reduction over the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034, the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House shall reduce\u2014\n**(1)**\nthe $4,500,000,000 reconciliation instruction for the Committee on Ways and Means under section 2001(b)(11);\n**(2)**\nthe allocations to the Committee on Ways and Means under section 302(a) of the Congressional Budget and Impoundment Control Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633(a) );\n**(3)**\nthe aggregates of budget authority, outlays, and revenues; and\n**(4)**\nany other appropriate level in this concurrent resolution,\nby an amount equal to the difference between $2,000,000,000,000 and the total dollar amount of such recommendations.\n##### (b) Adjustment if deficit reduction target exceeded\nIf one or more committees of the House of Representatives submit reconciliation recommendations pursuant to paragraphs (1), (3), (4), (5), (8), (9), or (10) of section 2001(b) and such recommendations, in total, achieve at least $2,000,000,000,000 in net deficit reduction over the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034, the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House shall increase the levels described in paragraphs (1) through (4) of subsection (a) by an amount equal to the difference between the total dollar amount of such recommendations and $2,000,000,000,000.\n##### (c) Certification required for adjustment\nNo adjustment may be made under subsection (a) or subsection (b) unless the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House, using cost estimates provided by the Congressional Budget Office and the Joint Committee on Taxation (as appropriate), certifies in writing that the applicable reconciliation recommendations\u2014\n**(1)**\nwith respect to subsection (a), do not achieve net deficit reduction of at least $2,000,000,000,000 over the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034; or\n**(2)**\nwith respect to subsection (b), achieve net deficit reduction of at least $2,000,000,000,000 over the period of such fiscal years.\n##### (d) Reconciliation instruction for Ways and Means\nThe dollar amount resulting from any adjustment made under this section to the reconciliation instruction for the Committee on Ways and Means under paragraph (11) of section 2001(b) shall be substituted for $4,500,000,000,000 in such section and shall be deemed the reconciliation instructions for such Committee under such section. Any recommendations on changes in law within the jurisdiction of the Committee shall be consistent with the goals of this concurrent resolution, including with respect to spending reduction, tax policy changes, reforms, or other measures deemed appropriate by the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House.\n##### (e) Consistency with the resolution\nAny reconciliation recommendations receiving an allocation adjustment under this section shall not be considered in violation of the budgetary levels established by this concurrent resolution.\nIV\nPolicy Statements\n#### 4001. Policy statement on economic growth\n##### (a) Findings\nThe House finds the following:\n**(1)**\nThe rate of economic growth has a significant impact on budget deficits. When the rate of gross domestic product (GDP) increases, projected revenue grows with it and deficits decline. Conversely, slower GDP growth can lead to lagging revenues and mounting deficits.\n**(2)**\nFederal policies affect the economy\u2019s potential to grow and impact economic performance, influencing budgetary outcomes. Consequently, fiscally responsible policies that improve the economy\u2019s long-term growth prospects help reduce the size of budget deficits over a given period.\n**(3)**\nThe free market, where individuals pursue their own self-interests, has been responsible for greater advancements in quality of life and generation of wealth than any other form of economic system. Federal policies designed to grow the economy should thus allow market forces to operate unhindered rather than pick winners and losers .\n##### (b) Policy on economic growth\nIt is the policy of this concurrent resolution to pursue policies that embrace the free market and promote economic growth policies that\u2014\n**(1)**\nreduce Federal spending;\n**(2)**\nexpand American energy production;\n**(3)**\nlower taxes that discourage work, savings, and investment;\n**(4)**\nderegulate the economy and enact reforms to diminish bureaucratic red tape; and\n**(5)**\neliminate barriers to work so more Americans enter (or reenter) the job market.\n#### 4002. Policy statement on mandatory spending reduction\n##### (a) Findings\nThe House finds the following:\n**(1)**\nThe United States faces a significant debt crisis, with the national debt currently exceeding $36 trillion, or 123 percent of GDP.\n**(2)**\nSince 2019, mandatory spending has increased by 59 percent.\n**(3)**\nThis debt poses a significant risk to the country's long-term fiscal sustainability, with implications for future generations.\n**(4)**\nMandatory spending currently accounts for over 70 percent of the entire Federal budget.\n**(5)**\nThe deficit for fiscal year 2025 is projected to be $1.9 trillion, or 6.2 percent of GDP.\n**(6)**\nThis fiscal year, net interest will total $952 billion, or 3.2 percent of GDP.\n##### (b) Policy on mandatory spending reduction\nIt is the goal of this concurrent resolution to reduce mandatory spending by $2 trillion over the budget window. If the combined deficit reduction provided by authorizing committees is below this target, it is the policy of the Committee on the Budget of the House that the instruction provided to the Committee on Ways and Means of the House should be reduced by a commensurate amount to offset the difference.\n#### 4003. Policy statement on Government deregulation\n##### (a) Findings\nThe House finds the following:\n**(1)**\nRegulations throughout the Federal Government have been a major issue for decades, continuously growing while negatively impacting the nation's economic and fiscal standing.\n**(2)**\nOverregulation has consistently hurt small businesses, strangled domestic energy production, weakened labor market conditions, and expanded government overreach and costs on taxpayers.\n**(3)**\nReal (inflation-adjusted) spending on regulatory agencies has increased exponentially since 1960. The total number of pages in the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) has increased from 22,877 pages in 1960 to nearly 200,000 today. When compared to 1950, the CFR contained only 9,745 pages in 1950, making the size of the CFR today 95% larger than it was in 1950.\n##### (b) Policy statement on Government deregulation\nIt is the policy of this concurrent resolution\u2014\n**(1)**\nthat Congress continues to examine ways to relieve the burdens of overregulation throughout the Federal Government;\n**(2)**\nthat Congress is ready to promote initiatives that will reduce government bureaucracy, enhance Federalism, and increase economic prosperity through deregulation;\n**(3)**\nto not only reduce burdensome, costly regulations, but to also reassert the role of Congress; and\n**(4)**\nto enact legislation through reconciliation that strengthens Congress, scales back Federal regulations, limits future bureaucratic red tape, and unleashes economic growth, such as the Regulations from the Executive in Need of Scrutiny (REINS) Act.\nV\nOther matters\n#### 5001. Enforcement filing in the House of Representatives\nIn the House of Representatives, if a concurrent resolution on the budget for fiscal year 2025 is adopted without the appointment of a committee of conference on the disagreeing votes of the two Houses with respect to this concurrent resolution on the budget, for the purpose of enforcing the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 621 et seq. ) and applicable rules and requirements set forth in the concurrent resolution on the budget, the allocations provided for in this section shall apply in the House of Representatives in the same manner as if such allocations were in a joint explanatory statement accompanying a conference report on the budget for fiscal year 2025. The chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House of Representatives shall submit a statement for publication in the Congressional Record containing\u2014\n**(1)**\nfor the Committee on Appropriations, committee allocations for fiscal year 2025 consistent with title I for the purpose of enforcing section 302 of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633 ); and\n**(2)**\nfor all committees other than the Committee on Appropriations, committee allocations consistent with title I for fiscal year 2025 and for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034 for the purpose of enforcing section 302 of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633 ).\n#### 5002. Budgetary treatment of administrative expenses in the House of Representatives\n##### (a) In general\nIn the House of Representatives, notwithstanding section 302(a)(1) of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633(a)(1) ), section 13301 of the Budget Enforcement Act of 1990 ( 2 U.S.C. 632 note), and section 2009a of title 39, United States Code, the report or the joint explanatory statement accompanying this concurrent resolution on the budget or the statement filed pursuant to section 5001 , as applicable, shall include in an allocation under section 302(a) of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633(a) ) to the Committee on Appropriations of the House of Representatives amounts for the discretionary administrative expenses of the Social Security Administration and the United States Postal Service.\n##### (b) Special rule\nIn the House of Representatives, for purposes of enforcing section 302(f) of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633(f) ), estimates of the level of total new budget authority and total outlays provided by a measure shall include any discretionary amounts described in subsection (a).\n#### 5003. Application and effect of changes in allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels\n##### (a) Application\nAny adjustments of allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels made pursuant to this concurrent resolution shall\u2014\n**(1)**\napply while that measure is under consideration;\n**(2)**\ntake effect upon the enactment of that measure; and\n**(3)**\nbe published in the Congressional Record as soon as practicable.\n##### (b) Effect of changed allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels\nRevised allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels resulting from these adjustments shall be considered for the purposes of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 621 et seq. ) as the allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels contained in this concurrent resolution.\n##### (c) Budget committee determinations\nFor purposes of this concurrent resolution, the levels of new budget authority, outlays, direct spending, new entitlement authority, revenues, deficits, and surpluses for a fiscal year or period of fiscal years shall be determined on the basis of estimates made by the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the applicable House of Congress.\n##### (d) Aggregates, allocations and application\nIn the House of Representatives, for purposes of this concurrent resolution and budget enforcement, the consideration of any bill or joint resolution, or amendment thereto or conference report thereon, for which the chair of the Committee on the Budget makes adjustments or revisions in the allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels of this concurrent resolution shall not be subject to the point of order set forth in clause 10 of rule XXI of the Rules of the House of Representatives.\n#### 5004. Adjustments to reflect changes in concepts and definitions in the House of Representatives\nIn the House of Representatives, the chair of the Committee on the Budget may adjust the appropriate aggregates, allocations, and other budgetary levels in this concurrent resolution for any change in budgetary concepts and definitions consistent with section 251(b)(1) of the Balanced Budget and Emergency Deficit Control Act of 1985 ( 2 U.S.C. 901(b)(1) ).\n#### 5005. Adjustment for changes in the baseline\nIn the House of Representatives, the chair of the Committee on the Budget may adjust the allocations, aggregates, and other appropriate budgetary levels in this concurrent resolution to reflect changes resulting from the Congressional Budget Office\u2019s updates to its baseline for fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n#### 5006. Exercise of rulemaking powers\nCongress adopts the provisions of this title\u2014\n**(1)**\nas an exercise of the rulemaking power of the Senate and the House of Representatives, respectively, and as such they shall be considered as part of the rules of each House or of that House to which they specifically apply, and such rules shall supersede other rules only to the extent that they are inconsistent with such other rules; and\n**(2)**\nwith full recognition of the constitutional right of either the Senate or the House of Representatives to change those rules (insofar as they relate to that House) at any time, in the same manner, and to the same extent as is the case of any other rule of the Senate or House of Representatives.",
      "versionDate": "2025-02-18",
      "versionType": "RH"
    },

      "text": "III\nCalendar No. 38\n119th CONGRESS\n1st Session\nH. CON. RES. 14\nIN THE SENATE OF THE UNITED STATES\nApril 2, 2025 Received and placed on the calendar\nCONCURRENT RESOLUTION\nEstablishing the congressional budget for the United States Government for fiscal year 2025 and setting forth the appropriate budgetary levels for fiscal years 2026 through 2034.\n#### 1. Concurrent resolution on the budget for fiscal year 2025\n##### (a) Declaration\nThe Congress determines and declares that prior concurrent resolutions on the budget are replaced as of fiscal year 2025 and that this concurrent resolution establishes the budget for fiscal year 2025 and sets forth the appropriate budgetary levels for fiscal years 2026 through 2034.\n##### (b) Table of contents\nThe table of contents for this concurrent resolution is as follows:\nSec. 1. Concurrent resolution on the budget for fiscal year 2025.\nTitle I\u2014Recommended Levels and Amounts\nSec. 1001. Recommended levels and amounts.\nSec. 1002. Major functional categories.\nTitle II\u2014Reconciliation and Related Matters\nSec. 2001. Reconciliation in the House of Representatives.\nTitle III\u2014Reserve Fund and Deficit Reduction Adjustment\nSec. 3001. Reserve fund for reconciliation legislation in the House of Representatives.\nSec. 3002. Adjustment for spending cuts of at least $2 trillion.\nTitle IV\u2014Policy Statements\nSec. 4001. Policy statement on economic growth.\nSec. 4002. Policy statement on mandatory spending reduction.\nSec. 4003. Policy statement on Government deregulation.\nTitle V\u2014Other matters\nSec. 5001. Enforcement filing in the House of Representatives.\nSec. 5002. Budgetary treatment of administrative expenses in the House of Representatives.\nSec. 5003. Application and effect of changes in allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels.\nSec. 5004. Adjustments to reflect changes in concepts and definitions in the House of Representatives.\nSec. 5005. Adjustment for changes in the baseline.\nSec. 5006. Exercise of rulemaking powers.\nI\nRecommended Levels and Amounts\n#### 1001. Recommended levels and amounts\nThe following budgetary levels are appropriate for each of fiscal years 2025 through 2034:\n**(1) Federal revenues**\nFor purposes of the enforcement of this concurrent budget authority, -$154,014,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, -$154,013,000,000.\nFiscal year 2032:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$160,114,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, -$160,113,000,000.\nFiscal year year 2026:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$4,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nFiscal year 2027:\n**(A)**\nNew budget authority, -$4,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nFiscal year 2028:\n**(A)**\nNew -$5,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nII\nReconciliation and Related Matters\n#### 2001. Reconciliation in the House of Representatives\n##### (a) Submissions\nNot later than March 27, 2025, the committees named in subsection (b) and subsection (c) shall submit their recommendations on changes in laws within their jurisdictions to the Committee on the Budget of the House of Representatives to carry out this section.\n##### (b) Instructions\n**(1) Committee on Agriculture**\nThe Committee on Agriculture shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $230,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(2) Committee on Armed Services**\nThe Committee on Armed Services shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the deficit by not more than $100,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(3) Committee on Education and Workforce**\nThe Committee on Education and Workforce shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $330,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(4) Committee on Energy and Commerce**\nThe Committee on Energy and Commerce shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $880,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(5) Committee on Financial Services**\nThe Committee on Financial Services shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $1,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(6) Committee on Homeland Security**\nThe Committee on Homeland Security shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the deficit by not more than $90,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(7) Committee on the Judiciary**\nThe Committee on the Judiciary shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the deficit by not more than $110,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(8) Committee on Natural Resources**\nThe Committee on Natural Resources shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $1,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(9) Committee on Oversight and Government Reform**\nThe Committee on Oversight and Government Reform shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $50,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(10) Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure**\nThe Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $10,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(11) Committee on Ways and Means**\nThe Committee on Ways and Means shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the deficit by not more than $4,500,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n##### (c) Increase in statutory debt limit\nThe Committee on Ways and Means shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the statutory debt limit by $4,000,000,000,000.\nIII\nReserve Fund and Deficit Reduction Adjustment\n#### 3001. Reserve fund for reconciliation legislation in the House of Representatives\n##### (a) In general\nIn the House of Representatives, the chair of the Committee on the Budget may revise the allocations of a committee or committees, aggregates, and other appropriate levels in this resolution for any bill or joint resolution considered pursuant to section 2001 containing the recommendations of one or more committees, or for one or more amendments to, a conference report on, or an amendment between the Houses in relation to such a bill or joint resolution, by the amounts necessary to accommodate the budgetary effects of the legislation, if the budgetary effects of the legislation comply with the reconciliation instructions under this concurrent resolution.\n##### (b) Determination of compliance\nFor purposes of this section, compliance with the reconciliation instructions under this concurrent resolution shall be determined by the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House of Representatives.\n#### 3002. Adjustment for spending cuts of at least $2 trillion\n##### (a) Adjustment if deficit reduction target not achieved\nIf one or more committees of the House of Representatives submit reconciliation recommendations pursuant to paragraphs (1), (3), (4), (5), (8), (9), or (10) of section 2001(b) and such recommendations do not, in total, achieve at least $2,000,000,000,000 in net deficit reduction over the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034, the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House shall reduce\u2014\n**(1)**\nthe $4,500,000,000,000 reconciliation instruction for the Committee on Ways and Means under section 2001(b)(11);\n**(2)**\nthe allocations to the Committee on Ways and Means under section 302(a) of the Congressional Budget and Impoundment Control Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633(a) );\n**(3)**\nthe aggregates of budget authority, outlays, and revenues; and\n**(4)**\nany other appropriate level in this concurrent resolution,\nby an amount equal to the difference between $2,000,000,000,000 and the total dollar amount of such recommendations.\n##### (b) Adjustment if deficit reduction target exceeded\nIf one or more committees of the House of Representatives submit reconciliation recommendations pursuant to paragraphs (1), (3), (4), (5), (8), (9), or (10) of section 2001(b) and such recommendations, in total, achieve at least $2,000,000,000,000 in net deficit reduction over the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034, the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House shall increase the levels described in paragraphs (1) through (4) of subsection (a) by an amount equal to the difference between the total dollar amount of such recommendations and $2,000,000,000,000.\n##### (c) Certification required for adjustment\nNo adjustment may be made under subsection (a) or subsection (b) unless the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House, using cost estimates provided by the Congressional Budget Office and the Joint Committee on Taxation (as appropriate), certifies in writing that the applicable reconciliation recommendations\u2014\n**(1)**\nwith respect to subsection (a), do not achieve net deficit reduction of at least $2,000,000,000,000 over the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034; or\n**(2)**\nwith respect to subsection (b), achieve net deficit reduction of at least $2,000,000,000,000 over the period of such fiscal years.\n##### (d) Reconciliation instruction for Ways and Means\nThe dollar amount resulting from any adjustment made under this section to the reconciliation instruction for the Committee on Ways and Means under paragraph (11) of section 2001(b) shall be substituted for $4,500,000,000,000 in such section and shall be deemed the reconciliation instructions for such Committee under such section. Any recommendations on changes in law within the jurisdiction of the Committee shall be consistent with the goals of this concurrent resolution, including with respect to spending reduction, tax policy changes, reforms, or other measures deemed appropriate by the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House.\n##### (e) Consistency with the resolution\nAny reconciliation recommendations receiving an allocation adjustment under this section shall not be considered in violation of the budgetary levels established by this concurrent resolution.\nIV\nPolicy Statements\n#### 4001. Policy statement on economic growth\n##### (a) Findings\nThe House finds the following:\n**(1)**\nThe rate of economic growth has a significant impact on budget deficits. When the rate of gross domestic product (GDP) increases, projected revenue grows with it and deficits decline. Conversely, slower GDP growth can lead to lagging revenues and mounting deficits.\n**(2)**\nFederal policies affect the economy\u2019s potential to grow and impact economic performance, influencing budgetary outcomes. Consequently, fiscally responsible policies that improve the economy\u2019s long-term growth prospects help reduce the size of budget deficits over a given period.\n**(3)**\nThe free market, where individuals pursue their own self-interests, has been responsible for greater advancements in quality of life and generation of wealth than any other form of economic system. Federal policies designed to grow the economy should thus allow market forces to operate unhindered rather than pick winners and losers .\n##### (b) Policy on economic growth\nIt is the policy of this concurrent resolution to pursue policies that embrace the free market and promote economic growth policies that\u2014\n**(1)**\nreduce Federal spending;\n**(2)**\nexpand American energy production;\n**(3)**\nlower taxes that discourage work, savings, and investment;\n**(4)**\nderegulate the economy and enact reforms to diminish bureaucratic red tape; and\n**(5)**\neliminate barriers to work so more Americans enter (or reenter) the job market.\n#### 4002. Policy statement on mandatory spending reduction\n##### (a) Findings\nThe House finds the following:\n**(1)**\nThe United States faces a significant debt crisis, with the national debt currently exceeding $36 trillion, or 123 percent of GDP.\n**(2)**\nSince 2019, mandatory spending has increased by 59 percent.\n**(3)**\nThis debt poses a significant risk to the country's long-term fiscal sustainability, with implications for future generations.\n**(4)**\nMandatory spending currently accounts for over 70 percent of the entire Federal budget.\n**(5)**\nThe deficit for fiscal year 2025 is projected to be $1.9 trillion, or 6.2 percent of GDP.\n**(6)**\nThis fiscal year, net interest will total $952 billion, or 3.2 percent of GDP.\n##### (b) Policy on mandatory spending reduction\nIt is the goal of this concurrent resolution to reduce mandatory spending by $2 trillion over the budget window. If the combined deficit reduction provided by authorizing committees is below this target, it is the policy of the Committee on the Budget of the House that the instruction provided to the Committee on Ways and Means of the House should be reduced by a commensurate amount to offset the difference.\n#### 4003. Policy statement on Government deregulation\n##### (a) Findings\nThe House finds the following:\n**(1)**\nRegulations throughout the Federal Government have been a major issue for decades, continuously growing while negatively impacting the nation's economic and fiscal standing.\n**(2)**\nOverregulation has consistently hurt small businesses, strangled domestic energy production, weakened labor market conditions, and expanded government overreach and costs on taxpayers.\n**(3)**\nReal (inflation-adjusted) spending on regulatory agencies has increased exponentially since 1960. The total number of pages in the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) has increased from 22,877 pages in 1960 to nearly 200,000 today. When compared to 1950, the CFR contained only 9,745 pages in 1950, making the size of the CFR today 95% larger than it was in 1950.\n##### (b) Policy statement on Government deregulation\nIt is the policy of this concurrent resolution\u2014\n**(1)**\nthat Congress continues to examine ways to relieve the burdens of overregulation throughout the Federal Government;\n**(2)**\nthat Congress is ready to promote initiatives that will reduce government bureaucracy, enhance Federalism, and increase economic prosperity through deregulation;\n**(3)**\nto not only reduce burdensome, costly regulations, but to also reassert the role of Congress; and\n**(4)**\nto enact legislation through reconciliation that strengthens Congress, scales back Federal regulations, limits future bureaucratic red tape, and unleashes economic growth, such as the Regulations from the Executive in Need of Scrutiny (REINS) Act.\nV\nOther matters\n#### 5001. Enforcement filing in the House of Representatives\nIn the House of Representatives, if a concurrent resolution on the budget for fiscal year 2025 is adopted without the appointment of a committee of conference on the disagreeing votes of the two Houses with respect to this concurrent resolution on the budget, for the purpose of enforcing the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 621 et seq. ) and applicable rules and requirements set forth in the concurrent resolution on the budget, the allocations provided for in this section shall apply in the House of Representatives in the same manner as if such allocations were in a joint explanatory statement accompanying a conference report on the budget for fiscal year 2025. The chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House of Representatives shall submit a statement for publication in the Congressional Record containing\u2014\n**(1)**\nfor the Committee on Appropriations, committee allocations for fiscal year 2025 consistent with title I for the purpose of enforcing section 302 of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633 ); and\n**(2)**\nfor all committees other than the Committee on Appropriations, committee allocations consistent with title I for fiscal year 2025 and for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034 for the purpose of enforcing section 302 of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633 ).\n#### 5002. Budgetary treatment of administrative expenses in the House of Representatives\n##### (a) In general\nIn the House of Representatives, notwithstanding section 302(a)(1) of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633(a)(1) ), section 13301 of the Budget Enforcement Act of 1990 ( 2 U.S.C. 632 note), and section 2009a of title 39, United States Code, the report or the joint explanatory statement accompanying this concurrent resolution on the budget or the statement filed pursuant to section 5001 , as applicable, shall include in an allocation under section 302(a) of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633(a) ) to the Committee on Appropriations of the House of Representatives amounts for the discretionary administrative expenses of the Social Security Administration and the United States Postal Service.\n##### (b) Special rule\nIn the House of Representatives, for purposes of enforcing section 302(f) of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633(f) ), estimates of the level of total new budget authority and total outlays provided by a measure shall include any discretionary amounts described in subsection (a).\n#### 5003. Application and effect of changes in allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels\n##### (a) Application\nAny adjustments of allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels made pursuant to this concurrent resolution shall\u2014\n**(1)**\napply while that measure is under consideration;\n**(2)**\ntake effect upon the enactment of that measure; and\n**(3)**\nbe published in the Congressional Record as soon as practicable.\n##### (b) Effect of changed allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels\nRevised allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels resulting from these adjustments shall be considered for the purposes of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 621 et seq. ) as the allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels contained in this concurrent resolution.\n##### (c) Budget committee determinations\nFor purposes of this concurrent resolution, the levels of new budget authority, outlays, direct spending, new entitlement authority, revenues, deficits, and surpluses for a fiscal year or period of fiscal years shall be determined on the basis of estimates made by the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the applicable House of Congress.\n##### (d) Aggregates, allocations and application\nIn the House of Representatives, for purposes of this concurrent resolution and budget enforcement, the consideration of any bill or joint resolution, or amendment thereto or conference report thereon, for which the chair of the Committee on the Budget makes adjustments or revisions in the allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels of this concurrent resolution shall not be subject to the point of order set forth in clause 10 of rule XXI of the Rules of the House of Representatives.\n#### 5004. Adjustments to reflect changes in concepts and definitions in the House of Representatives\nIn the House of Representatives, the chair of the Committee on the Budget may adjust the appropriate aggregates, allocations, and other budgetary levels in this concurrent resolution for any change in budgetary concepts and definitions consistent with section 251(b)(1) of the Balanced Budget and Emergency Deficit Control Act of 1985 ( 2 U.S.C. 901(b)(1) ).\n#### 5005. Adjustment for changes in the baseline\nIn the House of Representatives, the chair of the Committee on the Budget may adjust the allocations, aggregates, and other appropriate budgetary levels in this concurrent resolution to reflect changes resulting from the Congressional Budget Office\u2019s updates to its baseline for fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n#### 5006. Exercise of rulemaking powers\nCongress adopts the provisions of this title\u2014\n**(1)**\nas an exercise of the rulemaking power of the Senate and the House of Representatives, respectively, and as such they shall be considered as part of the rules of each House or of that House to which they specifically apply, and such rules shall supersede other rules only to the extent that they are inconsistent with such other rules; and\n**(2)**\nwith full recognition of the constitutional right of either the Senate or the House of Representatives to change those rules (insofar as they relate to that House) at any time, in the same manner, and to the same extent as is the case of any other rule of the Senate or House of Representatives.",

      "versionType": "PCS"
    },

      "text": "IV\n119th CONGRESS\n1st Session\nH. CON. RES. 14\nIN THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES\nCONCURRENT RESOLUTION\nEstablishing the congressional budget for the United States Government for fiscal year 2025 and setting forth the appropriate budgetary levels for fiscal years 2026 through 2034.\n#### 1. Concurrent resolution on the budget for fiscal year 2025\n##### (a) Declaration\nThe Congress determines and declares that prior concurrent resolutions on the budget are replaced as of fiscal year 2025 and that this concurrent resolution establishes the budget for fiscal year 2025 and sets forth the appropriate budgetary levels for fiscal years 2026 through 2034.\n##### (b) Table of contents\nThe table of contents for this concurrent resolution is as follows:\nSec.\u20021.\u2002Concurrent resolution on the budget for fiscal year 2025.\nTitle I\u2014Recommended Levels and Amounts\nSec.\u20021001.\u2002Recommended levels and amounts.\nSec.\u20021002.\u2002Major functional categories.\nTitle II\u2014Reconciliation and Related Matters\nSec.\u20022001.\u2002Reconciliation in the House of Representatives.\nTitle III\u2014Reserve Fund and Deficit Reduction Adjustment\nSec.\u20023001.\u2002Reserve fund for reconciliation legislation in the House of Representatives.\nSec.\u20023002.\u2002Adjustment for spending cuts of at least $2 trillion.\nTitle IV\u2014Policy Statements\nSec.\u20024001.\u2002Policy statement on economic growth.\nSec.\u20024002.\u2002Policy statement on mandatory spending reduction.\nSec.\u20024003.\u2002Policy statement on Government deregulation.\nTitle V\u2014Other matters\nSec.\u20025001.\u2002Enforcement filing in the House of Representatives.\nSec.\u20025002.\u2002Budgetary treatment of administrative expenses in the House of Representatives.\nSec.\u20025003.\u2002Application and effect of changes in allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels.\nSec.\u20025004.\u2002Adjustments to reflect changes in concepts and definitions in the House of Representatives.\nSec.\u20025005.\u2002Adjustment for changes in the baseline.\nSec.\u20025006.\u2002Exercise of rulemaking powers.\nI\nRecommended Levels and Amounts\n#### 1001. Recommended levels and amounts\nThe following budgetary levels are appropriate for each of fiscal years 2025 through 2034:\n**(1) Federal revenues**\nFor purposes of the enforcement of this concurrent resolution:\n**(A)**\nThe recommended levels of Federal revenues are as follows:\nFiscal year 2025: $3,408,969,000,000.\nFiscal year 2026: $3,766,668,000,000.\nFiscal year 2027: $4,066,393,000,000.\nFiscal year 2028: $4,186,847,000,000.\nFiscal year 2029: $4,309,831,000,000.\nFiscal year 2030: $4,508,641,000,000.\nFiscal year 2031: $4,730,270,000,000.\nFiscal year 2032: $4,938,712,000,000.\nFiscal year 2033: $5,172,643,000,000.\nFiscal year 2034: $5,410,030,000,000.\n**(B)**\nThe amounts by which the aggregate levels of Federal revenues should be changed are as follows:\nFiscal year 2025: -$450,000,000,000.\nFiscal year 2026: -$450,000,000,000.\nFiscal year 2027: -$450,000,000,000.\nFiscal year 2028: -$450,000,000,000.\nFiscal year 2029: $5,515,610,000,000.\nFiscal year 2026: $5,605,352,000,000.\nFiscal year 2027: $5,744,975,000,000.\nFiscal year 2028: $5,999,399,000,000.\nFiscal year 2029: $6,173,475,000,000.\nFiscal year 2030: $6,494,898,000,000.\nFiscal year 2031: $6,748,868,000,000.\nFiscal year 2032: $7,048,096,000,000.\nFiscal year 2033: $7,438,116,000,000.\nFiscal year 2034: $7,610,582,000,000.\n**(3) Budget outlays**\nFor purposes of the enforcement of this concurrent resolution, the appropriate levels of total budget outlays are as follows:\nFiscal year 2025: $5,490,790,000,000.\nFiscal year 2026: $5,623,085,000,000.\nFiscal year 2027: $5,821,621,000,000.\nFiscal year 2028: $6,088,332,000,000.\nFiscal year 2029: $6,164,293,000,000.\nFiscal year 2030: $6,484,545,000,000.\nFiscal year 2031: concurrent resolution, the amounts of the deficits are as follows:\nFiscal year 2025: $2,081,821,000,000.\nFiscal year 2026: $1,856,417,000,000.\nFiscal year 2027: $1,755,228,000,000.\nFiscal year 2028: $1,901,485,000,000.\nFiscal year 2029: $1,854,462,000,000.\nFiscal year 2030: $1,975,904,000,000.\nFiscal year 2031: $1,990,221,000,000.\nFiscal year 2032: $2,044,925,000,000.\nFiscal year 2033: $47,646,893,000,000.\nFiscal year 2031: $49,490,401,000,000.\nFiscal year 2032: $51,311,359,000,000.\nFiscal year 2033: $53,342,100,000,000.\nFiscal year 2034: $55,566,372,000,000.\n**(6) Debt held by the public**\nThe appropriate levels of debt held by the public are as follows:\nFiscal year 2025: $30,430,405,000,000.\nFiscal year 2026: $32,469,082,000,000.\nFiscal year 2027: -$5,000,000.\n**(B)**\nOutlays, $0.\nII\nReconciliation and Related Matters\n#### 2001. Reconciliation in the House of Representatives\n##### (a) Submissions\nNot later than March 27, 2025, the committees named in subsection (b) and subsection (c) shall submit their recommendations on changes in laws within their jurisdictions to the Committee on the Budget of the House of Representatives to carry out this section.\n##### (b) Instructions\n**(1) Committee on Agriculture**\nThe Committee on Agriculture shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $230,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(2) Committee on Armed Services**\nThe Committee on Armed Services shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the deficit by not more than $100,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(3) Committee on Education and Workforce**\nThe Committee on Education and Workforce shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $330,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(4) Committee on Energy and Commerce**\nThe Committee on Energy and Commerce shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $880,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(5) Committee on Financial Services**\nThe Committee on Financial Services shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $1,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(6) Committee on Homeland Security**\nThe Committee on Homeland Security shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the deficit by not more than $90,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(7) Committee on the Judiciary**\nThe Committee on the Judiciary shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the deficit by not more than $110,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(8) Committee on Natural Resources**\nThe Committee on Natural Resources shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $1,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(9) Committee on Oversight and Government Reform**\nThe Committee on Oversight and Government Reform shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $50,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(10) Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure**\nThe Committee on Transportation and Infrastructure shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction to reduce the deficit by not less than $10,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n**(11) Committee on Ways and Means**\nThe Committee on Ways and Means shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the deficit by not more than $4,500,000,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n##### (c) Increase in statutory debt limit\nThe Committee on Ways and Means shall submit changes in laws within its jurisdiction that increase the statutory debt limit by $4,000,000,000,000.\nIII\nReserve Fund and Deficit Reduction Adjustment\n#### 3001. Reserve fund for reconciliation legislation in the House of Representatives\n##### (a) In general\nIn the House of Representatives, the chair of the Committee on the Budget may revise the allocations of a committee or committees, aggregates, and other appropriate levels in this resolution for any bill or joint resolution considered pursuant to section 2001 containing the recommendations of one or more committees, or for one or more amendments to, a conference report on, or an amendment between the Houses in relation to such a bill or joint resolution, by the amounts necessary to accommodate the budgetary effects of the legislation, if the budgetary effects of the legislation comply with the reconciliation instructions under this concurrent resolution.\n##### (b) Determination of compliance\nFor purposes of this section, compliance with the reconciliation instructions under this concurrent resolution shall be determined by the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House of Representatives.\n#### 3002. Adjustment for spending cuts of at least $2 trillion\n##### (a) Adjustment if deficit reduction target not achieved\nIf one or more committees of the House of Representatives submit reconciliation recommendations pursuant to paragraphs (1), (3), (4), (5), (8), (9), or (10) of section 2001(b) and such recommendations do not, in total, achieve at least $2,000,000,000,000 in net deficit reduction over the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034, the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House shall reduce\u2014\n**(1)**\nthe $4,500,000,000,000 reconciliation instruction for the Committee on Ways and Means under section 2001(b)(11);\n**(2)**\nthe allocations to the Committee on Ways and Means under section 302(a) of the Congressional Budget and Impoundment Control Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633(a) );\n**(3)**\nthe aggregates of budget authority, outlays, and revenues; and\n**(4)**\nany other appropriate level in this concurrent resolution,\nby an amount equal to the difference between $2,000,000,000,000 and the total dollar amount of such recommendations.\n##### (b) Adjustment if deficit reduction target exceeded\nIf one or more committees of the House of Representatives submit reconciliation recommendations pursuant to paragraphs (1), (3), (4), (5), (8), (9), or (10) of section 2001(b) and such recommendations, in total, achieve at least $2,000,000,000,000 in net deficit reduction over the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034, the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House shall increase the levels described in paragraphs (1) through (4) of subsection (a) by an amount equal to the difference between the total dollar amount of such recommendations and $2,000,000,000,000.\n##### (c) Certification required for adjustment\nNo adjustment may be made under subsection (a) or subsection (b) unless the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House, using cost estimates provided by the Congressional Budget Office and the Joint Committee on Taxation (as appropriate), certifies in writing that the applicable reconciliation recommendations\u2014\n**(1)**\nwith respect to subsection (a), do not achieve net deficit reduction of at least $2,000,000,000,000 over the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034; or\n**(2)**\nwith respect to subsection (b), achieve net deficit reduction of at least $2,000,000,000,000 over the period of such fiscal years.\n##### (d) Reconciliation instruction for Ways and Means\nThe dollar amount resulting from any adjustment made under this section to the reconciliation instruction for the Committee on Ways and Means under paragraph (11) of section 2001(b) shall be substituted for $4,500,000,000,000 in such section and shall be deemed the reconciliation instructions for such Committee under such section. Any recommendations on changes in law within the jurisdiction of the Committee shall be consistent with the goals of this concurrent resolution, including with respect to spending reduction, tax policy changes, reforms, or other measures deemed appropriate by the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House.\n##### (e) Consistency with the resolution\nAny reconciliation recommendations receiving an allocation adjustment under this section shall not be considered in violation of the budgetary levels established by this concurrent resolution.\nIV\nPolicy Statements\n#### 4001. Policy statement on economic growth\n##### (a) Findings\nThe House finds the following:\n**(1)**\nThe rate of economic growth has a significant impact on budget deficits. When the rate of gross domestic product (GDP) increases, projected revenue grows with it and deficits decline. Conversely, slower GDP growth can lead to lagging revenues and mounting deficits.\n**(2)**\nFederal policies affect the economy\u2019s potential to grow and impact economic performance, influencing budgetary outcomes. Consequently, fiscally responsible policies that improve the economy\u2019s long-term growth prospects help reduce the size of budget deficits over a given period.\n**(3)**\nThe free market, where individuals pursue their own self-interests, has been responsible for greater advancements in quality of life and generation of wealth than any other form of economic system. Federal policies designed to grow the economy should thus allow market forces to operate unhindered rather than pick winners and losers .\n##### (b) Policy on economic growth\nIt is the policy of this concurrent resolution to pursue policies that embrace the free market and promote economic growth policies that\u2014\n**(1)**\nreduce Federal spending;\n**(2)**\nexpand American energy production;\n**(3)**\nlower taxes that discourage work, savings, and investment;\n**(4)**\nderegulate the economy and enact reforms to diminish bureaucratic red tape; and\n**(5)**\neliminate barriers to work so more Americans enter (or reenter) the job market.\n#### 4002. Policy statement on mandatory spending reduction\n##### (a) Findings\nThe House finds the following:\n**(1)**\nThe United States faces a significant debt crisis, with the national debt currently exceeding $36 trillion, or 123 percent of GDP.\n**(2)**\nSince 2019, mandatory spending has increased by 59 percent.\n**(3)**\nThis debt poses a significant risk to the country's long-term fiscal sustainability, with implications for future generations.\n**(4)**\nMandatory spending currently accounts for over 70 percent of the entire Federal budget.\n**(5)**\nThe deficit for fiscal year 2025 is projected to be $1.9 trillion, or 6.2 percent of GDP.\n**(6)**\nThis fiscal year, net interest will total $952 billion, or 3.2 percent of GDP.\n##### (b) Policy on mandatory spending reduction\nIt is the goal of this concurrent resolution to reduce mandatory spending by $2 trillion over the budget window. If the combined deficit reduction provided by authorizing committees is below this target, it is the policy of the Committee on the Budget of the House that the instruction provided to the Committee on Ways and Means of the House should be reduced by a commensurate amount to offset the difference.\n#### 4003. Policy statement on Government deregulation\n##### (a) Findings\nThe House finds the following:\n**(1)**\nRegulations throughout the Federal Government have been a major issue for decades, continuously growing while negatively impacting the nation's economic and fiscal standing.\n**(2)**\nOverregulation has consistently hurt small businesses, strangled domestic energy production, weakened labor market conditions, and expanded government overreach and costs on taxpayers.\n**(3)**\nReal (inflation-adjusted) spending on regulatory agencies has increased exponentially since 1960. The total number of pages in the Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) has increased from 22,877 pages in 1960 to nearly 200,000 today. When compared to 1950, the CFR contained only 9,745 pages in 1950, making the size of the CFR today 95% larger than it was in 1950.\n##### (b) Policy statement on Government deregulation\nIt is the policy of this concurrent resolution\u2014\n**(1)**\nthat Congress continues to examine ways to relieve the burdens of overregulation throughout the Federal Government;\n**(2)**\nthat Congress is ready to promote initiatives that will reduce government bureaucracy, enhance Federalism, and increase economic prosperity through deregulation;\n**(3)**\nto not only reduce burdensome, costly regulations, but to also reassert the role of Congress; and\n**(4)**\nto enact legislation through reconciliation that strengthens Congress, scales back Federal regulations, limits future bureaucratic red tape, and unleashes economic growth, such as the Regulations from the Executive in Need of Scrutiny (REINS) Act.\nV\nOther matters\n#### 5001. Enforcement filing in the House of Representatives\nIn the House of Representatives, if a concurrent resolution on the budget for fiscal year 2025 is adopted without the appointment of a committee of conference on the disagreeing votes of the two Houses with respect to this concurrent resolution on the budget, for the purpose of enforcing the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 621 et seq. ) and applicable rules and requirements set forth in the concurrent resolution on the budget, the allocations provided for in this section shall apply in the House of Representatives in the same manner as if such allocations were in a joint explanatory statement accompanying a conference report on the budget for fiscal year 2025. The chair of the Committee on the Budget of the House of Representatives shall submit a statement for publication in the Congressional Record containing\u2014\n**(1)**\nfor the Committee on Appropriations, committee allocations for fiscal year 2025 consistent with title I for the purpose of enforcing section 302 of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633 ); and\n**(2)**\nfor all committees other than the Committee on Appropriations, committee allocations consistent with title I for fiscal year 2025 and for the period of fiscal years 2025 through 2034 for the purpose of enforcing section 302 of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633 ).\n#### 5002. Budgetary treatment of administrative expenses in the House of Representatives\n##### (a) In general\nIn the House of Representatives, notwithstanding section 302(a)(1) of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633(a)(1) ), section 13301 of the Budget Enforcement Act of 1990 ( 2 U.S.C. 632 note), and section 2009a of title 39, United States Code, the report or the joint explanatory statement accompanying this concurrent resolution on the budget or the statement filed pursuant to section 5001 , as applicable, shall include in an allocation under section 302(a) of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633(a) ) to the Committee on Appropriations of the House of Representatives amounts for the discretionary administrative expenses of the Social Security Administration and the United States Postal Service.\n##### (b) Special rule\nIn the House of Representatives, for purposes of enforcing section 302(f) of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 633(f) ), estimates of the level of total new budget authority and total outlays provided by a measure shall include any discretionary amounts described in subsection (a).\n#### 5003. Application and effect of changes in allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels\n##### (a) Application\nAny adjustments of allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels made pursuant to this concurrent resolution shall\u2014\n**(1)**\napply while that measure is under consideration;\n**(2)**\ntake effect upon the enactment of that measure; and\n**(3)**\nbe published in the Congressional Record as soon as practicable.\n##### (b) Effect of changed allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels\nRevised allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels resulting from these adjustments shall be considered for the purposes of the Congressional Budget Act of 1974 ( 2 U.S.C. 621 et seq. ) as the allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels contained in this concurrent resolution.\n##### (c) Budget committee determinations\nFor purposes of this concurrent resolution, the levels of new budget authority, outlays, direct spending, new entitlement authority, revenues, deficits, and surpluses for a fiscal year or period of fiscal years shall be determined on the basis of estimates made by the chair of the Committee on the Budget of the applicable House of Congress.\n##### (d) Aggregates, allocations and application\nIn the House of Representatives, for purposes of this concurrent resolution and budget enforcement, the consideration of any bill or joint resolution, or amendment thereto or conference report thereon, for which the chair of the Committee on the Budget makes adjustments or revisions in the allocations, aggregates, and other budgetary levels of this concurrent resolution shall not be subject to the point of order set forth in clause 10 of rule XXI of the Rules of the House of Representatives.\n#### 5004. Adjustments to reflect changes in concepts and definitions in the House of Representatives\nIn the House of Representatives, the chair of the Committee on the Budget may adjust the appropriate aggregates, allocations, and other budgetary levels in this concurrent resolution for any change in budgetary concepts and definitions consistent with section 251(b)(1) of the Balanced Budget and Emergency Deficit Control Act of 1985 ( 2 U.S.C. 901(b)(1) ).\n#### 5005. Adjustment for changes in the baseline\nIn the House of Representatives, the chair of the Committee on the Budget may adjust the allocations, aggregates, and other appropriate budgetary levels in this concurrent resolution to reflect changes resulting from the Congressional Budget Office\u2019s updates to its baseline for fiscal years 2025 through 2034.\n#### 5006. Exercise of rulemaking powers\nCongress adopts the provisions of this title\u2014\n**(1)**\nas an exercise of the rulemaking power of the Senate and the House of Representatives, respectively, and as such they shall be considered as part of the rules of each House or of that House to which they specifically apply, and such rules shall supersede other rules only to the extent that they are inconsistent with such other rules; and\n**(2)**\nwith full recognition of the constitutional right of either the Senate or the House of Representatives to change those rules (insofar as they relate to that House) at any time, in the same manner, and to the same extent as is the case of any other rule of the Senate or House of Representatives.",
      "versionDate": "",
      "versionType": "EH"
    },
